questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: What is the scale of a standard sectional aeronautical chart?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states sectional charts have a scale of 1:500,000 (1 inch = about 6.86 NM), allowing detailed information to be shown.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Groundspeed differs from true airspeed in what way?
options: Groundspeed is true airspeed adjusted for wind
Groundspeed is always higher than true airspeed
Groundspeed is true airspeed corrected for temperature
Groundspeed and true airspeed are always equal
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines groundspeed as the actual speed over the ground - true airspeed adjusted for wind; it decreases with a headwind and increases with a tailwind.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Magnetic variation is the angle between:
questions.explain: Variation is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north at a location.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Compass deviation is caused by:
questions.explain: Deviation is the compass error produced by the aircraft’s own electrical and metallic components.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: One degree of latitude equals approximately:
questions.explain: One degree of latitude is about 60 nautical miles (1 minute ≈ 1 NM).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A VOR provides the pilot with:
questions.explain: A VOR gives bearing information as radials from the station; DME adds distance.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Isogonic lines on a chart connect points of equal:
questions.explain: Isogonic lines join points of equal magnetic variation (an agonic line has zero variation).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Dead-reckoning navigation estimates position using:
questions.explain: Dead reckoning computes position from a known start using heading, speed, wind and elapsed time.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A great circle route between two points represents —
options: The shortest distance between them along the Earth's surface
questions.explain: A great circle is the shortest path between two points on a sphere; a rhumb line (constant heading) is usually longer.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) provides the pilot with —
questions.explain: DME gives slant-range distance from the aircraft to the ground station, often paired with a VOR for bearing.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: An ADF (Automatic Direction Finder) needle points toward —
options: The tuned NDB station, regardless of aircraft heading
questions.explain: An ADF needle points at the tuned NDB station's relative bearing, independent of the aircraft's heading reference.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The variation in compass reading caused by an aircraft's own magnetic and electrical equipment is corrected for using a —
questions.explain: A compass deviation card, mounted near the compass, lists correction values for the aircraft's own magnetic interference at various headings.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

banks.title: Flight Planning & Fuel
banks.desc: Mass & balance, fuel reserves, performance and the ISA — the planning numbers.
banks.source: GACAR Part 91 · FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: On a standard day (ISA), what is the sea-level temperature?
questions.explain: The ICAO Standard Atmosphere defines sea level as 15°C and 1013.25 hPa.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: What is the approximate ISA temperature lapse rate in the troposphere?
questions.explain: Temperature falls about 2°C (1.98°C) per 1,000 ft up to the tropopause.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: How does a high density altitude affect take-off performance?
questions.explain: Thinner air reduces engine thrust and lift, lengthening the take-off roll and flattening the climb.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Loading an aircraft aft of its centre-of-gravity limit mainly affects what?
questions.explain: An aft CG reduces longitudinal stability and can make stall recovery difficult.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Final reserve fuel is best described as fuel that…
options: may be used during the cruise
should never be planned for use
questions.explain: Final reserve is a protected minimum on landing — it is never planned to be consumed en route.

questions.q: One nautical mile is approximately how many metres?
questions.explain: By definition 1 NM = 1,852 m (1,609 m is a statute mile).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: With a true airspeed of 120 kt and a 20 kt direct headwind, the groundspeed is…
questions.explain: A headwind subtracts from TAS: 120 − 20 = 100 kt groundspeed.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: To convert a fuel volume in litres to a mass, you multiply by its…
questions.explain: Mass = volume × density. Avgas density is roughly 0.72 kg/L.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A stronger headwind component has what effect on take-off and landing distance?
questions.explain: A headwind reduces the ground run required for both take-off and landing.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Density altitude equals pressure altitude when…
options: the temperature equals ISA for that level
questions.explain: When the actual temperature matches the ISA value for that level, density altitude equals pressure altitude.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Pressure altitude is the altitude read when the altimeter sub-scale is set to:
questions.explain: Pressure altitude is indicated when the altimeter is set to the standard 1013.25 hPa / 29.92 inHg.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The crosswind component is greatest when the wind is:
questions.explain: A wind at 90° to the runway is entirely crosswind; aligned with the runway it is all headwind.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Increasing aircraft weight will:
questions.explain: Greater weight increases both take-off and landing distance and raises the stall speed.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: On a hot, high-elevation aerodrome, density altitude is:
questions.explain: High temperature raises density altitude above pressure altitude, degrading performance.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A VFR flight must plan to carry fuel to the destination plus:
questions.explain: Fuel planning must include trip fuel plus the required reserves under GACAR Part 91.

questions.q: Contingency (or extra) fuel is carried to allow for:
options: Unforeseen factors such as wind or re-routing
questions.explain: Contingency fuel covers unforeseen deviations from the plan, such as stronger winds or re-routing.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: For a fixed indicated airspeed, true airspeed:
questions.explain: As air density falls with altitude, true airspeed is higher than indicated airspeed for the same IAS.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A wind correction angle is applied to the:
options: Heading, to maintain the desired track
questions.explain: The pilot turns the heading into wind by the wind correction angle to track the desired course.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The E6B flight computer (mechanical or electronic) is primarily used to calculate —
options: Time, speed, distance, fuel and wind-related values for flight planning
questions.explain: The E6B is a flight-planning calculator for time/speed/distance, fuel, and wind-triangle problems.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Weight and balance calculations before flight ensure the aircraft is operated —
options: Within its certificated weight and center-of-gravity limits
questions.explain: Pre-flight weight and balance planning keeps the aircraft within its certificated weight and CG envelope for safe handling.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A NOTAM should be checked during flight planning because it may report —
options: Temporary hazards or changes such as runway closures or navaid outages
questions.explain: Preflight action requires familiarity with all available information, and NOTAMs carry time-critical changes (closures, outages, hazards) not yet reflected on charts.

questions.q: "Top of descent" in flight planning is the point calculated so the aircraft —
options: Reaches the desired altitude/position at the planned descent rate
Always occurs directly over the destination airport
questions.explain: Top-of-descent planning uses a target descent rate/gradient so the aircraft reaches the desired point and altitude at the right time.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

banks.title: Commercial Pilot Licensing
banks.desc: CPL eligibility, aeronautical experience, privileges and limitations under GACAR Part 61.
banks.source: GACAR Part 61, Subpart F · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, the minimum age to be eligible for a commercial pilot certificate is —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(a) requires a commercial pilot certificate applicant to be at least 18 years of age.

questions.q: Before being issued a commercial pilot certificate, an applicant must already hold at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(e) requires the applicant to hold at least a private pilot certificate issued under Part 61 (or meet the requirements of §61.93).

questions.q: Which class of medical certificate must a commercial pilot certificate applicant hold (for airplane operations)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(g) requires a Class 1 medical certificate for a commercial pilot certificate; only glider or balloon applicants may hold a Class 2.

questions.q: A commercial pilot certificate applicant needs only a Class 2 medical certificate when testing for or exercising the privileges in a —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(g) makes an exception to the Class 1 requirement: persons undergoing the practical test for or exercising commercial privileges in a glider or balloon need only a Class 2 medical certificate.

questions.q: Which pilot certificate is the minimum required to act as pilot in command of an aircraft carrying persons or property for compensation or hire?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(a)(2) grants the commercial pilot certificate holder the privilege to act as PIC carrying persons or property for compensation or hire, making it the minimum certificate for paid operations.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.155, a commercial pilot certificate holder may exercise the same privileges as a person holding a —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(a)(1) provides that a commercial pilot may exercise the same privileges as a private pilot, in addition to the compensation-or-hire privileges.

questions.q: A commercial pilot who applies with an airplane category rating and does not hold an instrument rating in the same category and class is limited from carrying passengers for hire on cross-country flights in excess of —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(b)(1) imposes the limitation that carriage of passengers for hire on cross-country flights in excess of 50 NM (or at night) is prohibited without the corresponding instrument rating.

questions.q: Besides cross-country flights over 50 NM, the no-instrument-rating commercial limitation also prohibits carrying passengers for hire —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(b)(1) prohibits carriage of passengers for hire on cross-country flights over 50 NM or at night until an instrument rating is obtained.

questions.q: How is the commercial pilot's 50 NM / night carriage-for-hire limitation removed?
options: By obtaining an instrument rating in the same category and class
By passing a separate night checkride
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(b)(1) states the limitation may be removed once the person satisfactorily accomplishes the §61.89 requirements for an instrument rating in the same category and class listed on the certificate.

questions.q: GACAR §61.151 states that this subpart prescribes the requirements for the issuance of which certificates?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.151 (Applicability) provides that the subpart prescribes the requirements, conditions, privileges, and limitations for commercial pilot certificates and ratings.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.3, a person may not exercise the privileges of a certificate issued under Part 61 if that certificate is —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.3(a) prohibits exercising the privileges of a certificate, rating, endorsement, or authorization that has been surrendered, suspended, revoked, or expired.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.3, a commercial or airline transport pilot may not serve as a pilot in international operations under Part 121 or 135 after attaining their 60th birthday, or, in operations with more than one pilot, their —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.3(c) bars a CPL or ATP holder from serving as a pilot in such international operations after the 60th birthday, extended to the 65th birthday in multi-pilot operations.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.5, to exercise the privileges of a certificate a person must have in their physical possession or readily accessible in the aircraft —
options: A current and valid airman certificate
questions.explain: GACAR §61.5(a) requires a current and valid airman certificate (or §61.37(c) document / Temporary Flight Authorization) endorsed with the necessary ratings, in possession or readily accessible in the aircraft.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.9, the medical certificate that a required flight crew member must hold is issued under GACAR Part —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.9(a) requires a required flight crew member to hold the appropriate medical certificate issued under GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a pilot certificate issued under GACAR §61.7?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.7(a) lists the pilot certificates as student, sport, private, commercial, and ATP; aircraft dispatcher is not a pilot certificate under this part.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.7, instrument ratings are placed on which pilot certificates?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.7(b)(8) specifies that instrument ratings are placed on private and commercial pilot certificates only.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.153, the commercial pilot knowledge test is based on the aeronautical knowledge areas listed in an appendix to GACAR Part —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(b) requires passing the knowledge test on the aeronautical knowledge areas listed in Appendix C to GACAR Part 141.

questions.q: For an applicant taking the logged ground-and-flight-training route to a commercial pilot certificate, the required aeronautical experience is prescribed in which appendix to GACAR Part 61?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(c)(4) requires that such applicants meet the aeronautical experience requirements of Appendix D to Part 61.

banks.title: Commercial Air Operations
banks.desc: Air operator certification and the operating rules for commercial flights (GACAR Parts 119, 135, 91).

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 119, a person authorized to conduct operations as a commercial operator is issued which certificate?
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 states that a person authorized to conduct operations as a commercial operator under Part 121 or 135 is issued an Air Operator Certificate (AOC); noncommercial operators under Part 125 receive an Operator Certificate (OC).

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 119, a person authorized to conduct noncommercial operations under GACAR Part 125 is issued —
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 provides that a person authorized to conduct noncommercial operations under Part 125 will be issued an Operator Certificate (OC), as distinct from the AOC issued to commercial operators.

questions.q: GACAR Part 119 prescribes the certification requirements for operations conducted under which Parts?
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 states that Part 119 prescribes the certification requirements an operator must meet to obtain and hold a certificate authorizing operations under Part 121, 125, or 135.

questions.q: According to GACAR §119.1, Part 119 does NOT apply to which of the following?
options: Foreign air carriers operating under GACAR Part 129
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 expressly states that Part 119 does not apply to activities conducted by foreign air carriers under Part 129, nor to aerial work operations under Part 133.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.1, no person may operate as a commercial operator under Part 119 without also holding —
options: Appropriate economic authority from the GACA
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 provides that no person may operate as a commercial operator under this part without holding appropriate economic authority from the GACA, in addition to the AOC and operations specifications.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.5, a certificate holder's operations specifications must contain —
options: The authorizations, limitations, and certain procedures under which each kind of operation is conducted
A list of passenger fares by route
The maintenance records of every engine
questions.explain: GACAR §119.5 requires that operations specifications contain the authorizations, limitations, and certain procedures under which each kind of operation is to be conducted, plus procedures for how each class of aircraft is to be operated.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.9, a certificate holder must not operate an aircraft under Part 121 or 135 using a business name other than —
options: The name of its principal pilot
A business name appearing in its operations specifications
Any name approved by the aircraft lessor
questions.explain: GACAR §119.9 prohibits a certificate holder from operating under a business name other than one appearing in its operations specifications.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.9, how must the certificate holder's business name relate to the aircraft it operates under Part 121 or 135?
options: It need not appear anywhere on the aircraft
It must be legibly displayed and clearly visible from outside the aircraft to a person on the ground
It must be shown only on the flight plan
It must be painted on the interior cabin only
questions.explain: GACAR §119.9 requires the operating certificate holder's business name to be legibly displayed on the aircraft and clearly visible and readable from outside to a person standing on the ground (except during flight time).

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.9, when a certificate holder operates a wet-leased aircraft under Part 121 or 135, whose business name must be legibly displayed on the aircraft?
options: No name is required for wet leases
questions.explain: GACAR §119.9 requires that for a wet-leased aircraft, the business name of the lessor be legibly displayed and clearly visible from outside the aircraft to a person standing on the ground.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.43, an application for an AOC may be denied if the President finds that —
options: The applicant has fewer than five aircraft
The applicant previously held an AOC or OC that was revoked
The applicant operates only within the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
The applicant intends to conduct only cargo operations
questions.explain: GACAR §119.43 lists grounds for denying a certificate, including that the applicant previously held an AOC or OC that was revoked, or is not properly and adequately equipped to conduct safe operations.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.43, before issuing an AOC the President must find that the applicant is properly and adequately equipped in accordance with the requirements of —
questions.explain: GACAR §119.43 requires the President to find that the applicant is properly and adequately equipped in accordance with GACAR Part 121 or 135, as applicable, and able to conduct a safe operation, before issuing an AOC.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.49, the operations specifications of a certificate holder conducting commercial operations must include —
options: The specific location of the certificate holder's principal base of operations
A copy of every passenger's ticket
The daily fuel prices at each aerodrome
questions.explain: GACAR §119.49 requires operations specifications to contain, among other items, the specific location of the certificate holder's principal base of operations, other business names, reference to the economic authority, and the aircraft authorized for use.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.37, to operate as a commercial operator a person must, among other things —
options: Be a citizen of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and maintain a principal base of operations in the Kingdom
Own at least one transport-category aircraft
Be based outside the Kingdom for tax purposes
questions.explain: GACAR §119.37 provides that a person may not operate as a commercial operator unless they are a citizen of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, maintain a principal base of operations in the Kingdom, and obtain an AOC.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.39, an applicant for an AOC must submit the application to the President at least —
options: 30 working days before the date of intended operation
60 working days before the date of intended operation
90 working days before the date of intended operation
15 working days before the date of intended operation
questions.explain: GACAR §119.39 requires each applicant to submit the AOC application at least 90 working days before the date of intended operation.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.21, a person conducting commercial operations using aircraft other than transport-category or commuter-category airplanes must comply with the certification requirements of Part 119 and —
questions.explain: GACAR §119.21 provides that commercial operators using transport-category aircraft or commuter-category airplanes follow Part 121, while those using aircraft not so addressed must comply with Part 119 and Part 135.

questions.q: GACAR §135.1 prescribes rules governing the commercial operations of —
options: Aircraft other than transport-category aircraft and commuter-category airplanes, by holders of an AOC under Part 119
questions.explain: GACAR §135.1 states that Part 135 governs the scheduled and unscheduled commercial operations of aircraft OTHER than transport-category aircraft and commuter-category airplanes, conducted by persons holding or required to hold an AOC under Part 119.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.7, in addition to the documents required by Part 91, the pilot in command must ensure which document is carried on board?
options: A certified true copy of the AOC and a copy of the operations specifications summary
The original design drawings of the aircraft
A copy of every crew member's pilot logbook
questions.explain: GACAR §135.7 requires the PIC to ensure that a certified true copy of the AOC and a copy of the operations specifications summary (and, when applicable, a copy of the Article 83 bis agreement) are carried on board.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.9, for how long must the flight logbook records be maintained?
questions.explain: GACAR §135.9 requires that flight logbook records be maintained for 30 days, and makes the PIC responsible for ensuring the entries are completed per the certificate holder's procedures.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.15, a single-engine airplane may be operated in instrument meteorological conditions (IMC) or at night only if, among other conditions —
options: The flight remains within 25 NM of the departure aerodrome
The certificate holder is authorized by the President to conduct such operations
At least two pilots are on board regardless of type
The airplane carries a parachute for each occupant
questions.explain: GACAR §135.15 permits single-engine airplane operations in IMC or at night only when several conditions are met, including that the certificate holder is specifically authorized by the President to conduct such operations.

questions.q: GACAR §91.1 establishes that Part 91 prescribes rules governing the operation of aircraft —
options: Only outside the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
Within the sovereign area and territorial waters of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
questions.explain: GACAR §91.1 states that Part 91 prescribes the general operating and flight rules for aircraft operated within the sovereign area and territorial waters of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, with certain exceptions.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.3, the pilot in command of an aircraft is —
options: Subordinate to the flight dispatcher in all decisions
Directly responsible for, and the final authority as to, the operation, safety, and security of that aircraft
Responsible only for navigation, not safety
questions.explain: GACAR §91.3 makes the PIC directly responsible for, and the final authority as to, the operation, safety, and security of the aircraft and the safety of all crew members, passengers, and cargo on board, and permits deviation from the rules to the extent required in an in-flight emergency.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.9, which of the following must the PIC ensure is carried on board a Saudi Arabian registered aircraft?
options: An original or true copy of the aircraft certificate of registration and the airworthiness certificate
A printed weather forecast for the next seven days
A signed copy of the certificate holder's business plan
questions.explain: GACAR §91.9 requires the PIC to ensure documents such as the aircraft certificate of registration, the airworthiness certificate, the radio station license, a passenger list, a cargo manifest, and the flight logbook are carried on board at all times.

banks.title: Commercial Performance & Loading
banks.desc: Weight, balance, performance and fuel requirements for commercial operations (GACAR Part 91 · 135).
banks.source: GACAR Part 91 · Part 135 (Subpart F) · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 91, an airplane may not begin a flight under VFR during the day unless it has enough fuel to reach the first point of intended landing and then fly at normal cruising speed for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.161(a) requires, for a daytime VFR airplane flight, fuel to reach the first point of intended landing plus at least 30 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.q: For a night VFR flight in an airplane, GACAR §91.161 requires fuel to reach the first point of intended landing and then fly at normal cruising speed for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.161(a) sets the night VFR airplane reserve at 45 minutes at normal cruising speed beyond the first point of intended landing (versus 30 minutes by day).

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.161, a rotorcraft may not begin a VFR flight unless it can reach the first point of intended landing and then fly at normal cruising speed for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.161(b) sets a single 20-minute reserve for rotorcraft under VFR, regardless of day or night.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 91, an airplane operating under IFR must carry enough fuel to fly to and make an approach at the destination, fly to the alternate, and then fly at normal cruising speed for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(a) requires an IFR airplane to carry fuel for the destination approach/missed approach, the flight to the alternate, and then 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.181, after reaching the alternate aerodrome an IFR rotorcraft must carry fuel to fly at normal cruising speed for a further —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(a)(3) gives rotorcraft a 30-minute final reserve at normal cruising speed under IFR, compared with 45 minutes for other aircraft.

questions.q: GACAR §91.43 (Preflight Action) requires the PIC, before a flight not in the vicinity of an aerodrome or under IFR, to become familiar with — among other things —
options: only the weather at the departure aerodrome
runway lengths at aerodromes of intended use and the applicable takeoff and landing distance data
nothing additional if the flight is conducted under VFR
questions.explain: GACAR §91.43(a) lists required preflight information including weather, fuel, alternatives, and runway lengths plus takeoff/landing distance data at aerodromes of intended use.

questions.q: Before a flight may commence under GACAR §91.43, the PIC must be satisfied that —
options: the aircraft mass and centre of gravity allow the flight to be conducted safely and any load is properly distributed and secured
the aircraft has flown within the preceding 30 days
the aircraft has been repainted within 12 months
questions.explain: GACAR §91.43(b) requires the PIC to confirm, before flight, that the aircraft's mass and CG permit safe flight, that any load is properly distributed and secured, and that operating limitations will not be exceeded.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.11, a Saudi-registered aircraft's current empty mass and centre of gravity must be derived from an actual weighing performed within the preceding —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.11(a) requires empty mass and CG to be calculated from an actual weighing within the preceding 36 months (unless an approved mass-and-balance control system or another exception applies).

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.267 (In-Flight Fuel Management), the PIC must declare a fuel emergency by broadcasting "MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY FUEL" when —
options: the aircraft has burned half of its planned trip fuel
the calculated usable fuel on landing at the nearest suitable aerodrome would be less than the planned final reserve fuel
the destination alternate is more than one hour away
questions.explain: GACAR §91.267 requires a MAYDAY FUEL emergency declaration when the calculated usable fuel predicted on landing at the nearest safe aerodrome is less than the planned final reserve fuel; a MINIMUM FUEL advisory precedes that state.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.263, when an aircraft operating within controlled airspace needs to dump fuel, the PIC must —
options: advise ATC before commencing the fuel-dump operation
obtain written approval from the operator
questions.explain: GACAR §91.263 requires the PIC to advise ATC prior to commencing a fuel-dump operation when operating within controlled airspace.

questions.q: In GACAR Part 135, airplane performance operating limitations (takeoff, en-route, and landing limits) for commercial operators are prescribed in —
questions.explain: GACAR Part 135 Subpart F is titled "Airplane Performance Operating Limitations"; Subpart G covers the equivalent rotorcraft limitations.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.135, when ensuring an airplane's takeoff mass does not exceed the mass allowed for the runway length, the operator must consider at least aerodrome pressure altitude, wind, ambient temperature, runway slope, and —
options: runway contaminants such as standing water, snow, or ice
the time since the last engine overhaul
questions.explain: GACAR §135.135(f) lists the required factors — pressure altitude, wind, ambient temperature, runway slope, and runway contaminants (standing water, snow, or ice).

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.137 (Airplane: Takeoff Limitations), the accelerate-stop distance must not exceed —
options: the length of the runway plus any stopway
the runway length plus any clearway
questions.explain: GACAR §135.137(b)(1) requires the accelerate-stop distance to be no greater than the length of the runway plus the length of any stopway.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.137, the takeoff distance must not exceed the runway length plus any clearway, but the clearway credited must not be greater than —
questions.explain: GACAR §135.137(b)(2) allows the takeoff distance to include clearway, but the clearway credited may not exceed one-half of the runway length.

questions.q: For the takeoff limitations of GACAR §135.137, it is assumed the airplane is not banked before reaching a height of 50 ft (15 m), and thereafter the maximum bank does not exceed —
questions.explain: GACAR §135.137(d) assumes no bank before 50 ft (15 m) and a maximum bank of 15 degrees thereafter along the takeoff/net takeoff flight path.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.139 (Landing Limitations: Destination Aerodromes), takeoff mass must be limited so that, allowing for fuel and oil burn, the airplane can make a full-stop landing —
options: within the landing distance available of the most suitable runway at the destination
within 70 percent of any runway at the destination
using no more than half the runway in still air
regardless of wind direction on the longest runway only
questions.explain: GACAR §135.139(a) limits mass so the airplane can make a full-stop landing within the landing distance available of the most suitable runway at the destination aerodrome.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.139, if forecasts indicate the destination runways may be wet or slippery and no wet-runway AFM data is used, a turbojet-powered airplane requires an effective runway length of at least —
options: 100 percent of the dry-runway required length
115 percent of the dry-runway required length
70 percent of the dry-runway required length
150 percent of the dry-runway required length
questions.explain: GACAR §135.139(c) requires, for a turbojet landing on possibly wet/slippery runways without applicable AFM wet data, an effective runway length of at least 115 percent of that required by paragraph (a).

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.141, an aerodrome may not be listed as an alternate unless the airplane can land and stop within — of the effective runway length from a point 50 ft above the obstruction-clearance-plane/runway intersection.
questions.explain: GACAR §135.141 requires an alternate to permit a full-stop landing within 70 percent of the effective runway length, using the §135.139 assumptions.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.143, a multi-engine land airplane carrying passengers over water (beyond gliding distance of land) must be operated at a mass allowing it to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §135.143(c) requires a multi-engine land airplane over water to be able to climb at least 50 ft/min (0.25 m/s) with the critical engine inoperative at 1,000 ft (300 m) AGL.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.145, a multi-engine airplane carrying passengers over the top or in IMC must be able to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least 50 ft/min when operating at —
options: the route MEA or 5,000 ft MSL, whichever is higher
the route MEA or 1,500 ft MSL, whichever is lower
questions.explain: GACAR §135.145(a) requires a 50 ft/min single-engine-inoperative climb capability at the minimum en-route IFR altitude (MEA) or 5,000 ft MSL, whichever is higher.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.658 (Fuel and Oil Supply), the contingency fuel carried to compensate for unforeseen factors must be no less than —
options: 5 percent of the planned trip fuel (subject to a minimum holding allowance)
10 percent of the planned trip fuel
15 percent of the final reserve fuel
questions.explain: GACAR §135.658 sets contingency fuel at no less than 5 percent of the planned trip fuel (or from the point of in-flight re-planning), never below the fuel to fly 5 minutes at holding speed 1,500 ft above the destination.

banks.title: IFR Flight Rules
banks.desc: Instrument flight rules: clearances, minima, fuel and alternate requirements under GACAR Part 91.
banks.source: GACAR Part 91, Subpart B/E · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.181, after flying to the first aerodrome of intended landing and then to the alternate, an aeroplane operating under IFR must carry enough fuel to fly for a further —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(a)(3) requires an aeroplane under IFR to carry fuel to reach the destination and alternate and then fly a further 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.q: For a rotorcraft operating under IFR, the final reserve fuel required by GACAR §91.181 (after destination and alternate) is enough to fly for —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(a)(3) sets the rotorcraft final reserve at 30 minutes at normal cruising speed, versus 45 minutes for other aircraft.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.183, no person may operate an aircraft in controlled airspace under IFR unless the PIC has —
options: Filed a VFR flight plan and obtained a weather briefing
An instrument rating and a working transponder only
Filed an IFR flight plan and received an appropriate ATC clearance
questions.explain: GACAR §91.183 requires both a filed IFR flight plan and receipt of an appropriate ATC clearance before operating IFR in controlled airspace.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.135, each person operating an aircraft in Class A airspace must —
options: Operate under either VFR or IFR
Operate under VFR with an ATC clearance
questions.explain: GACAR §91.135 requires all operations in Class A airspace to be conducted under IFR, under an ATC clearance received before entering the airspace.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.135, operations in Class A airspace may be conducted only under an ATC clearance received —
options: At any time during the flight
questions.explain: GACAR §91.135(a) states that Class A operations may be conducted only under an ATC clearance received prior to entering the airspace.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.77, an aircraft must be operated under IFR whenever it is at or above —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.77 makes IFR mandatory for any aircraft operating at flight level 150 or above.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.181, an alternate aerodrome need not be included in the IFR fuel calculation for an aeroplane if a Part 97 approach is published at the destination and, for at least 1 hour before and after ETA, the ceiling and visibility will be at least —
options: 2,000 ft above the aerodrome elevation and 5 km
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(b) waives the alternate-fuel requirement for aeroplanes when a Part 97 approach exists and forecasts show a ceiling at least 2,000 ft above aerodrome elevation and visibility at least 5 km for the hour before and after ETA.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.185, an alternate aerodrome must be listed in an IFR flight plan unless —
options: The flight is shorter than 100 NM
A Part 97 instrument approach is available at the destination and forecast weather meets the specified ceiling/visibility minima
The pilot holds an airline transport pilot licence
questions.explain: GACAR §91.185(b) allows omission of an alternate only when an approved Part 97 approach is published for the destination and the forecast ceiling and visibility meet the stated minimums around ETA.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.185(c), the standard alternate-aerodrome minimums for a precision approach are —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.185(c) sets the standard precision-approach alternate minimums (for aircraft other than rotorcraft) at a 600 ft ceiling and 3,200 m visibility.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.185(c), the standard alternate-aerodrome minimums for a nonprecision approach or APV are —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.185(c) sets the standard nonprecision/APV alternate minimums (for aircraft other than rotorcraft) at an 800 ft ceiling and 3,200 m visibility.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.187, a VOR used for IFR navigation must have been operationally checked within the preceding —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.187(a)(2) permits IFR use of a VOR only if it was operationally checked within the preceding 30 days and found within tolerance.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.187, the maximum permissible indicated bearing error when checking VOR equipment against a ground test signal or a designated surface checkpoint is —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.187(b) sets the maximum permissible bearing error at ±4° when using a ground test signal or a designated VOR surface checkpoint.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.187, when a VOR is checked against a designated airborne checkpoint, the maximum permissible bearing error is —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.187(b)(3) allows a wider ±6° tolerance when using an airborne VOR checkpoint, compared with ±4° for ground checks.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.187, when checking one VOR system against another (dual VOR), the maximum permissible variation between the two indicated bearings is —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.187(c) permits checking one VOR against the other tuned to the same station, with a maximum permissible variation of 4° between the two indicated bearings.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.199, over a designated mountainous zone where no minimum altitude is prescribed, the minimum IFR altitude is —
options: 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
2,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
1,500 ft above the highest obstacle within 5 NM of course
2,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 5 NM of course
questions.explain: GACAR §91.199(a)(2)(i) requires, over mountainous terrain with no prescribed minimum, an altitude of 2,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of the course flown.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.199, over non-mountainous terrain where no minimum altitude is prescribed, the minimum IFR altitude is —
options: 500 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 5 NM of course
2,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
questions.explain: GACAR §91.199(a)(2)(ii) requires, in non-mountainous areas with no prescribed minimum, an altitude of 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of the course flown.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.201, a PIC in level cruising flight under IFR in controlled airspace must maintain —
options: The semicircular cruising altitude for the magnetic course
The altitude or flight level assigned by ATC
questions.explain: GACAR §91.201(a) requires a PIC operating IFR in controlled airspace to maintain the altitude or flight level assigned by ATC.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.201, under IFR in uncontrolled airspace below 13,000 ft MSL on a magnetic course of 0° through 179°, the PIC must fly —
options: The altitude last assigned by ATC
questions.explain: GACAR §91.201(b) requires, in uncontrolled airspace below 13,000 ft MSL, any odd thousand-foot MSL altitude on magnetic courses of 0° through 179°.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.205, when making an initial approach to a radio navigation facility under IFR, the PIC may not descend below the pertinent minimum altitude for initial approach until —
options: Arrival over that facility has been definitely established
questions.explain: GACAR §91.205 prohibits descent below the minimum initial-approach altitude until arrival over the facility has been definitely established.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.209, if two-way radio communications fail under IFR and VMC are encountered, the PIC should —
options: Continue to the destination on the flight plan regardless of conditions
Continue the flight under VFR and land at the nearest suitable aerodrome
Climb to FL 150 and enter a holding pattern
questions.explain: GACAR §91.209(b) directs a PIC who loses radio in, or encounters, VMC to continue under VFR and land at the nearest suitable aerodrome, reporting arrival to ATS.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.211, a change from IFR to VFR flight —
options: May be initiated by ATC on the pilot's behalf
May be initiated only by the PIC, by stating 'CANCELING MY IFR FLIGHT'
Is prohibited once the aircraft is airborne
Requires filing a new flight plan on the ground first
questions.explain: GACAR §91.211(b) provides that only the PIC may initiate a change from IFR to VFR, by stating 'CANCELING MY IFR FLIGHT' along with any flight-plan changes.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.193, if the PIC has not served 100 hours as PIC in the type being flown, the landing minimums (MDA/DA and visibility) are increased by —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.193(a) increases the MDA or DA/DH by 100 ft and the visibility minimum by 800 m for a PIC with fewer than 100 hours as PIC in that aircraft type.

banks.title: Instrument Procedures
banks.desc: Approaches, holding and instrument procedures (GACAR Part 97 and the Saudi AIP ENR).
banks.source: GACAR Part 97 · Saudi AIP (ENR) · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: GACAR Part 97 exists primarily to —
options: Identify the approved instrument flight procedures (IFPs) authorized for use in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
questions.explain: §97.1 states that Part 97 identifies the approved instrument flight procedures (including instrument approach procedures) authorized for use within the territorial limits of the KSA.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.1, the rules governing the development, validation and approval of instrument flight procedures are addressed under —
questions.explain: §97.1(d) provides that development, validation, approval, promulgation and maintenance of IFPs are addressed under GACAR Part 172, not within Part 97.

questions.q: Per GACAR §97.1, the publication of instrument flight procedures in the KSA AIP and their depiction on aeronautical charts are governed by —
questions.explain: §97.1(e) states the rules for publishing IFPs in the KSA AIP and depicting them on charts are addressed under GACAR Part 175.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.3, an aircraft approach category is a grouping based on a speed of —
options: Vref (or 1.3 Vso if Vref is not specified) at the maximum certificated landing mass
Vso at the maximum takeoff mass
V2 at the maximum landing mass
questions.explain: §97.3(a) defines the aircraft approach category by a speed of Vref, or, if Vref is not specified, 1.3 Vso at the maximum certificated landing mass.

questions.q: Under the aircraft approach categories in GACAR §97.3, Category A applies to a speed of —
questions.explain: §97.3(a)(1) assigns Category A to aircraft with an approach speed of less than 91 knots indicated airspeed.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.3, aircraft approach Category C covers a speed of —
options: 166 knots IAS or more but less than 211 knots IAS
questions.explain: §97.3(a)(3) defines Category C as 121 knots or more but less than 141 knots IAS.

questions.q: Per the approach segment definitions in GACAR §97.3, the initial approach segment lies between the initial approach fix and —
options: The intermediate fix (or the point where the aircraft is established on the intermediate course/final approach course)
questions.explain: §97.3(c)(1) defines the initial approach segment as running from the initial approach fix to the intermediate fix, or to where the aircraft is established on the intermediate or final approach course.

questions.q: In GACAR §97.3, the final approach segment of an instrument approach procedure is the segment in which —
options: The aircraft climbs after a missed approach
Alignment and descent for landing are accomplished
The aircraft is vectored to the initial approach fix
questions.explain: §97.3(c)(4) describes the final approach segment as the segment in which alignment and descent for landing are accomplished.

questions.q: According to GACAR §97.3, the missed approach segment of an instrument approach procedure starts at the —
questions.explain: §97.3(c)(5) states the missed approach segment starts at the missed approach point and comprises an initial, intermediate and final phase.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.3, the intermediate phase of the missed approach extends from the start of climb to the point where obstacle clearance of at least — is first obtained and can be maintained.
questions.explain: §97.3(c)(5)(ii) defines the intermediate phase as extending from the start of climb to where 50 m (164 ft) of obstacle clearance is first obtained and can be maintained.

questions.q: Per GACAR §97.3, a procedure turn is a maneuver in which a turn is made away from a designated track followed by a turn in the opposite direction to —
options: Enter a holding pattern at the fix
Permit the aircraft to intercept and proceed along the reciprocal of the designated track
Establish the aircraft on the missed approach
questions.explain: §97.3(d) defines a procedure turn as a turn away from a designated track and then a turn in the opposite direction so the aircraft can intercept and proceed along the reciprocal of that track.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.5, all bearings, courses, tracks, headings and radials in the instrument procedures of Part 97 are expressed in —
questions.explain: §97.5(a) provides that all bearings, courses, tracks, headings and radials in Part 97 are in degrees magnetic unless otherwise designated.

questions.q: In the instrument procedures of GACAR Part 97, runway visual range (RVR) values are stated in —
questions.explain: §97.5(b) states RVR values are stated in meters, while other mileages are stated in nautical miles or applicable SI units.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.11, approved IFPs (including instrument approach and departure procedures) in the KSA are developed and maintained by —
options: Air navigation service providers certificated under GACAR Part 170 providing IFP services under Part 172
questions.explain: §97.11(a) provides that approved IFPs are developed and maintained by air navigation service providers certificated under GACAR Part 170 and providing instrument flight procedure services under GACAR Part 172.

questions.q: According to the Saudi AIP, the transition level (TL) for the whole JEDDAH FIR has been fixed by GACA at —
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 (Transition level) states the TL has been determined by GACA as fixed at FL 150 for the whole of JEDDAH FIR.

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, the fixed transition level of FL 150 in JEDDAH FIR is also described as the —
options: Standard cruising level for all IFR flights
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 notes that the transition level, fixed at FL 150, is therefore also the lowest usable flight level for the JEDDAH FIR.

questions.q: Under the Saudi AIP cruising-level rules, en-route cruising levels are expressed in flight levels at and above —
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 (Cruising levels) states levels are expressed as altitude at and below 13,000 FT AMSL, or as flight level at and above FL 150.

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, the appropriate flight information service (FIS) frequency for an aerodrome is published —
options: Only in the GEN section of the AIP
At the top of all instrument approach charts
On the en-route chart margins only
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 notes that the appropriate FIS frequency is published at the top of all instrument approach charts.

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, a pilot who has not received a landing clearance approaching short final shall —
options: Continue and land, then advise the tower
Request a landing clearance, and if not in receipt of one commence a missed approach not later than the runway landing threshold
Enter a holding pattern over the threshold
Reduce to minimum speed and orbit on final
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 states a landing clearance shall be requested approaching short final, and pilots not in receipt of one shall commence a missed approach not later than the runway landing threshold.

questions.q: Under the Saudi AIP general speed limits, aircraft operated below ALT 10,000 FT anywhere in JEDDAH FIR must not exceed an indicated airspeed of —
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 (Speed limits) sets a maximum of 250 KT IAS below 10,000 FT elsewhere in the FIR (with a lower limit inside the aerodrome traffic circuit).

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, the highest altitude at which a VFR flight may operate within JEDDAH FIR is —
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.2 (Maximum level for VFR) states the highest altitude at which a VFR flight may operate within JEDDAH FIR is 12,500 FT.

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, an aircraft joining the aerodrome traffic circuit from the same side as the traffic pattern shall normally —
options: Join straight in on the base leg
Arrange flight to join straight in on the downwind leg
Overfly the aerodrome at circuit height then join crosswind
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 states aircraft approaching from the same side as the traffic pattern shall arrange flight to join straight in on the downwind leg (those from the opposite side cross over the aerodrome at midfield first).

banks.title: Instrument Rating Requirements
banks.desc: Eligibility, experience and currency for the instrument rating under GACAR Part 61.
banks.source: GACAR Part 61 (Instrument Rating) · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, to apply for an instrument rating a person must, at minimum —
options: Hold at least a current private pilot certificate (or be concurrently applying for one) with an appropriate aircraft rating
Hold only a student pilot certificate
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(1) requires an instrument-rating applicant to hold at least a current private pilot certificate — or be concurrently applying for one — with an airplane, helicopter, or powered-lift rating appropriate to the instrument rating sought.

questions.q: Under §61.89, an instrument-rating applicant who does not already hold one must pass a knowledge test on the aeronautical knowledge areas of —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(2) requires the applicant (unless already instrument-rated) to pass the knowledge test on the aeronautical knowledge areas of Appendix B to GACAR Part 141.

questions.q: Under §61.89, no person may act as PIC of a civil aircraft under IFR, or in weather less than the minimums for VFR flight, unless that person holds —
options: An appropriate category/class/type rating and an instrument rating (or equivalent ATP privileges)
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(b) bars acting as PIC under IFR or in below-VFR weather unless the pilot holds the appropriate category, class, and type rating plus an instrument rating for the aircraft (or the equivalent ATP privileges).

questions.q: To act as PIC under IFR, §61.17 requires that within the preceding period the pilot have performed and logged, among other tasks, at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) requires, for instrument recent experience, at least six instrument approaches (plus holding procedures and course tracking) performed solely by reference to instruments.

questions.q: Under §61.17, the instrument approaches, holding, and course-tracking tasks required to act as PIC under IFR (in an aircraft) must have been performed within the preceding —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3)(i) sets the instrument recent-experience window at the 6 months preceding the month of the flight.

questions.q: Which task is part of the instrument recent experience §61.17 requires to act as PIC under IFR?
options: Intercepting and tracking courses using electronic navigation systems
A biennial flight review in a glider
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) lists intercepting and tracking courses using electronic navigation systems (along with six instrument approaches and holding procedures) as an instrument recent-experience task.

questions.q: Under §61.17, a pilot who has not met the instrument experience requirements for more than six calendar months may regain instrument currency only by —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(4) provides that once the instrument experience lapse exceeds six calendar months, currency can be reestablished only by completing an instrument proficiency check.

questions.q: Under §61.17, an instrument proficiency check must consist of —
options: The areas of operation and instrument tasks required in the instrument rating practical test standards
A short oral quiz on weather theory
The night takeoff and landing recency tasks
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(4) states the instrument proficiency check must consist of the areas of operation and instrument tasks required in the instrument rating practical test standards.

questions.q: Under §61.17, an instrument proficiency check may be given by all of the following EXCEPT —
options: Any private pilot who happens to hold an instrument rating
An authorized company check pilot under GACAR Part 121, 125, or 135
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(4)(ii) lists who may give the check — an examiner, an authorized instructor, an authorized company check pilot, or a person approved by the President — but not simply any instrument-rated private pilot.

questions.q: Under §61.89, if instrument-rating privileges are to be exercised in multi-engine aircraft, the flight training must include operating the aircraft solely by reference to instruments —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(c) requires that multi-engine instrument training include operating the aircraft of the appropriate category solely by reference to instruments with one engine inoperative.

questions.q: Under §61.89, a person holding or applying for an instrument rating on a private pilot certificate must hold a Class 1 medical certificate or —
options: Obtain a waiver from the President
Have established hearing acuity meeting the Class 1 medical hearing requirements
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(d) requires either a Class 1 medical certificate or established hearing acuity based on compliance with the hearing requirements for issue of a Class 1 medical certificate.

questions.q: Under §61.89, to act as PIC of a glider under IFR a person must hold a pilot certificate with a glider category rating and —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(b)(3) allows a glider to be flown under IFR by a person holding a pilot certificate with a glider category rating and an airplane instrument rating.

questions.q: Under §61.19, a person may serve as SIC of an aircraft under IFR only if, among other requirements, that person holds —
options: An instrument rating or privilege applicable to the aircraft being flown
A type rating in every aircraft in the fleet
questions.explain: GACAR §61.19(a)(2) requires an SIC to hold an instrument rating, or privilege applicable to the aircraft being flown, when the flight is conducted under IFR.

questions.q: Under §61.89, an instrument-rating applicant must pass a practical test on the areas of operation prescribed in —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(4) requires the applicant to pass the practical test on the areas of operation set out in Appendix B to GACAR Part 141.

questions.q: Under §61.17, when instrument recent experience is accomplished in an aviation training device (ATD), the required tasks must have been performed within the preceding —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) provides a shorter window for ATD-based instrument recency: the required tasks (including 3 hours and six approaches) must be within the 2 months preceding the month of the flight.

questions.q: Under §61.89, which is an acceptable way to meet the instrument-rating training requirement?
options: Simply holding a valid medical certificate
Graduating from an approved instrument rating course under Appendix B to GACAR Part 141
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(3) lists acceptable training routes, including a certificate of graduation from an approved instrument rating certification course under Appendix B to GACAR Part 141.

questions.q: The instrument recent-experience tasks in §61.17 must be performed —
options: Solely by reference to instruments, in actual or simulated instrument flight conditions
Only in actual IMC with a safety pilot
Only in a full flight simulator
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) requires the recent-experience tasks to be performed solely by reference to instruments, in actual or simulated instrument flight conditions.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, an instrument rating is required in order to —
options: Carry passengers for hire in day VFR
Act as PIC under IFR or in weather below the minimums for VFR flight
Fly a single-engine airplane in day VFR
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(b) makes an instrument rating a prerequisite to acting as PIC under IFR or in weather conditions less than the minimums prescribed for VFR flight.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the recent-experience tasks §61.17 requires to act as PIC under IFR?
options: A power-off stall series in VMC
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) lists holding procedures and tasks, along with six instrument approaches and course tracking, as instrument recent-experience requirements.

questions.q: Under §61.17, an instrument proficiency check (for other than a glider) may be conducted in an aircraft appropriate to the category or in —
options: An FSTD representative of the aircraft category
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(4)(i) allows the instrument proficiency check to be conducted in an aircraft appropriate to the category or, for other than a glider, in an FSTD that is representative of the aircraft category.

questions.q: Under §61.89, an applicant for an instrument rating who does not yet hold a private pilot certificate may —
options: Apply for the instrument rating concurrently with the private pilot certificate
Never be issued an instrument rating
Skip the private pilot requirement entirely
Obtain it only after logging 1,500 hours
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(1) permits an applicant to be concurrently applying for the private pilot certificate (with the appropriate aircraft rating) while seeking the instrument rating.

banks.title: Airline Transport Pilot Licensing
banks.desc: ATP certificate eligibility, aeronautical experience, privileges and limitations (GACAR Part 61, Subpart G).
banks.source: GACAR Part 61, Subpart G · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Which pilot certificate is governed by GACAR Part 61, Subpart G?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.171 states that Subpart G prescribes the requirements for the issuance of airline transport pilot (ATP) certificates and ratings, the conditions under which they are necessary, and their privileges and limitations.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, the minimum age to be eligible for an airline transport pilot (ATP) certificate is —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(a) requires an ATP certificate applicant to be at least 23 years of age.

questions.q: Which class of medical certificate must an applicant generally hold to exercise the privileges of an ATP certificate?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(i) requires an ATP applicant to hold a Class 1 medical certificate (with a limited glider/balloon exception permitting a Class 2).

questions.q: For an ATP certificate with an airplane category and class rating, what is the minimum total time as a pilot required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a) requires at least 1,500 hours of total time as a pilot for an ATP certificate with an airplane category and class rating.

questions.q: The aeronautical experience for an ATP airplane rating must include at least how many hours of cross-country flight time?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(1) requires at least 500 hours of cross-country flight time within the 1,500-hour total for an ATP airplane rating.

questions.q: How many hours of night flight time are required for an ATP certificate with an airplane category and class rating?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(2) requires at least 100 hours of night flight time for an ATP airplane rating.

questions.q: The ATP airplane aeronautical experience requirement includes at least how many hours of instrument flight time in actual or simulated instrument conditions?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(4) requires at least 75 hours of instrument flight time in actual or simulated instrument conditions for an ATP airplane rating.

questions.q: For an ATP airplane rating, at least how many hours of flight time must be as pilot in command (or as SIC performing PIC duties under supervision)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(5) requires at least 250 hours of flight time in an airplane as PIC, or as SIC performing the duties of a PIC under supervision, for an ATP airplane rating.

questions.q: For an ATP certificate with a rotorcraft category and helicopter class rating, what is the minimum total time as a pilot required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.183(a) requires at least 1,200 hours of total time as a pilot for an ATP certificate with a rotorcraft category and helicopter class rating.

questions.q: For an ATP certificate with a powered-lift category rating, what is the minimum total time as a pilot required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.185(a) requires at least 1,500 hours of total time as a pilot for an ATP certificate with a powered-lift category rating.

questions.q: In multi-pilot international operations under GACAR Part 121 or 135, a pilot holding an ATP certificate may not serve as a pilot after attaining which birthday?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.3(c) bars a commercial or ATP certificate holder from serving as a pilot in international Part 121/135 operations after the 60th birthday, or the 65th birthday in the case of operations with more than one pilot.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.175, when is an applicant NOT required to pass a knowledge test to add an aircraft type rating to an ATP certificate?
options: When the ATP certificate already lists the aircraft category and class rating appropriate to the type rating sought
Never — a knowledge test is always required
When the applicant is over 40 years of age
When the applicant holds a foreign private pilot licence
questions.explain: GACAR §61.175(b) waives the knowledge test for an additional aircraft type rating when the applicant's ATP certificate already lists the aircraft category and class rating appropriate to the type rating sought.

questions.q: The ATP certification training program for an airplane multiengine class rating requires at least how many hours of academic (classroom) instruction?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.177(a) requires at least 30 hours of classroom instruction in the ATP certification training program for an airplane multiengine class rating.

questions.q: Within the ATP certification training program (airplane multiengine), how many hours of flight-simulation training device (FSTD) training are required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.177(b) requires at least 10 hours of training in an FSTD representing a multiengine turbine airplane as part of the ATP certification training program.

questions.q: For an ATP airplane rating, at least how many hours must be flown in the specific class of airplane for the rating sought?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(3) requires at least 50 hours of flight time in the class of airplane for the rating sought (with limited FFS credit).

questions.q: For an ATP rotorcraft/helicopter rating, the 100 hours of required night flight time must include at least how many hours in helicopters?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.183(a)(2) requires 100 hours of night flight time, of which at least 15 hours are in helicopters, for an ATP rotorcraft/helicopter rating.

questions.q: Which of the following is a valid eligibility path to an ATP certificate under GACAR §61.173?
options: Holding a commercial pilot certificate with an instrument rating
Holding a private pilot certificate only
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(c) requires the applicant to hold a commercial pilot certificate with an instrument rating (or a qualifying foreign ATP licence with instrument privileges).

questions.q: For an ATP rotorcraft/helicopter rating, at least how many hours of cross-country flight time are required?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.183(a)(1) requires at least 500 hours of cross-country flight time for an ATP certificate with a rotorcraft category and helicopter class rating.

questions.q: For an ATP powered-lift rating, at least how many hours must be flown in a powered-lift as PIC (or as SIC performing PIC duties under supervision)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.185(a)(3) requires at least 250 hours in a powered-lift as PIC, or as SIC performing the duties of a PIC under supervision, for an ATP powered-lift rating.

questions.q: For the ATP airplane rating, not more than how many hours of the total aeronautical experience may be obtained in a full flight simulator or flight training device (in an approved course)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.181(a)(6) allows not more than 100 hours of the total aeronautical experience to be obtained in a full flight simulator or flight training device representing an airplane, when accomplished in an approved Part 121/135/141/142 course.

questions.q: Besides the minimum age, which is an eligibility requirement for an ATP certificate under GACAR §61.173?
options: Be currently employed by an airline
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(b) requires an ATP certificate applicant to be of good moral character, in addition to the age and other requirements.

questions.q: To be eligible for an ATP certificate with an airplane multiengine class rating, GACAR §61.173(d) requires the applicant to present —
options: A graduation certificate for the ATP certification training program (§61.177)
A university degree in aeronautical engineering
A type rating in a widebody aircraft
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173(d) requires, for an airplane multiengine class ATP rating, a graduation certificate from an authorized training provider certifying completion of the ATP certification training program specified in §61.177 before taking the knowledge test.

banks.title: Air Transport Operations
banks.desc: Operating rules for transport-category commercial air operators under GACAR Part 121.

questions.q: GACAR Part 121 prescribes rules governing the scheduled and unscheduled operations of a commercial air operator operating which category of aircraft?
options: Transport category airplanes/rotorcraft or commuter category airplanes
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1 applies Part 121 to commercial air operators operating transport category airplanes, transport category rotorcraft, or commuter category airplanes that hold or must hold an Air Operator Certificate.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 121, a certificate holder must hold or be required to hold an Air Operator Certificate (AOC) issued under which GACAR Part?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1 ties Part 121 operations to holding an Air Operator Certificate under GACAR Part 119; a certificate holder is defined as a Part 119 AOC holder operating under this part.

questions.q: For the purposes of GACAR Part 121, the term 'special unscheduled' distinguishes requirements applicable to aircraft with a type-certificated passenger seating capacity of —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.5 uses 'special unscheduled' for unscheduled operations applicable only to aircraft with a type-certificated passenger seating capacity of 56 seats or less, excluding required crew members.

questions.q: For scheduled and unscheduled operations, which of the following is NOT one of the full-time management positions a Part 121 certificate holder must staff?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.45(a) requires a director of safety, director of operations, chief pilot, director of maintenance, and chief inspector; a director of training is not among the listed required positions.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.45, a certificate holder must notify the President of any change in required management personnel or any vacancy within how many working days?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.45(f)(3) requires the certificate holder to notify the President within 10 working days of any change in personnel or any vacancy in a listed position.

questions.q: A certificate holder conducting only special unscheduled operations must staff which management positions under GACAR §121.45?
options: Director of operations, chief pilot, and director of maintenance
Director of safety and chief inspector only
Chief pilot and chief inspector only
All five positions required for scheduled operations
questions.explain: GACAR §121.45(b) requires special unscheduled operators to have a director of operations, a chief pilot, and a director of maintenance — a reduced set compared with scheduled operations.

questions.q: To serve as director of operations for a Part 121 certificate holder, a person must hold at minimum which pilot certificate?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.49(a) requires the director of operations to hold an airline transport pilot (ATP) certificate, plus specified supervisory and PIC experience.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.49, to serve as chief inspector a person must hold a mechanic certificate with both airframe and powerplant ratings and have held those ratings for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.49(d) requires the chief inspector to hold a mechanic certificate with airframe and powerplant ratings and to have held those ratings for at least 3 years.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.13, if a certificate holder knowingly permits an owned or leased aircraft to be engaged in an operation violating the psychoactive-substances rule, that operation is a basis for —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.13 provides that permitting an aircraft to be used in violation of GACAR §91.23 (carriage of psychoactive substances) is a basis for suspending or revoking the certificate.

questions.q: Beyond the general Part 91 documents, GACAR §121.17 requires a Part 121 certificate holder to carry on board —
options: A true copy of the AOC and a copy of the operations specifications
A copy of the certificate holder's financial statements
The original AOC and original operations specifications only
questions.explain: GACAR §121.17 requires carrying a true copy of the AOC and a copy of the operations specifications (and, when applicable, a true copy of the Article 83bis agreement).

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.139, each Part 121 certificate holder must prepare and keep current which manuals?
options: An operations manual and a maintenance manual
A marketing manual and a safety manual
questions.explain: GACAR §121.139(a) requires the certificate holder to prepare and keep current both an operations manual and a maintenance manual for the use and guidance of appropriate personnel.

questions.q: Among the requirements of GACAR §121.139, each required manual must —
options: Be available in the English language
Be revised no more than once per year
questions.explain: GACAR §121.139(c) requires each manual to be available in the English language, be easy to revise, reference applicable GACAR, and include human factors principles.

questions.q: GACAR §121.143 lists the content areas that the operations manual must contain. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.143(a) organizes operations-manual contents under General; Aircraft operating information; Areas, routes, and aerodromes; and Training — revenue accounting is not a required content area.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.147, a certificate holder must keep a current approved Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM), or approved equivalent, for each type of aircraft —
options: At its principal base of operations and in each aircraft of that type
Only in the certificate holder's legal department
Only aboard the aircraft during international flights
questions.explain: GACAR §121.147(a) requires a current approved AFM or approved equivalent at the principal base of operations and in each aircraft of that type that the holder operates.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.151, copies of the required manual (or appropriate parts) must be furnished to all of the following EXCEPT —
options: Appropriate ground operations and maintenance personnel
The GACA representatives assigned to it
questions.explain: GACAR §121.151(a) requires furnishing manuals to ground operations and maintenance personnel, crew members, and assigned GACA representatives; passengers are not on that list.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.517, no person may take off an aircraft with inoperable instruments or equipment installed unless —
options: The PIC gives verbal approval before departure
An approved minimum equipment list (MEL) exists and the operations specifications authorize operations under it
The item is scheduled for repair within 10 days
A passenger briefing on the defect is given
questions.explain: GACAR §121.517(a) permits takeoff with inoperable items only when an approved MEL exists and the operations specifications authorize operation under that MEL, with the aircraft operated under its conditions and limitations.

questions.q: Which of the following may NOT be included in a certificate holder's approved MEL under GACAR §121.517?
options: Instruments or equipment required by an airworthiness directive to be operable
questions.explain: GACAR §121.517(b) bars the MEL from including instruments/equipment required by an airworthiness directive to be operable, items required by the type-certification airworthiness requirements, and items required for specific operations by Part 121 and Part 91.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1301, a certificate holder conducting scheduled operations must have which operational-control system?
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1301 requires scheduled operators to have a dispatch release system; unscheduled operators may use either a flight release system or a dispatch release system.

questions.q: Under the dispatch release system of GACAR §121.1309, no person may continue a flight from an intermediate aerodrome without re-dispatch if the aircraft has been on the ground more than —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1309(a) prohibits continuing a flight from an intermediate aerodrome without re-dispatch once the aircraft has been on the ground more than 6 hours.

questions.q: Under the dispatch release system of GACAR §121.1309, who are jointly responsible for the preflight planning, delay, and dispatch release of a flight?
options: The PIC and the aircraft dispatcher
The chief pilot and the director of safety
The GACA and the certificate holder
The first officer and the cabin crew
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1309(a)(4) makes the PIC and the aircraft dispatcher jointly responsible for the preflight planning, delay, and dispatch release, in compliance with Part 121, the operations specifications, and the manual.

questions.q: Under the flight release system of GACAR §121.1325, operational control for each flight must be shared between the PIC and —
questions.explain: GACAR §121.1325(a) requires operational control for each flight to be shared between the PIC and the director of operations, who may delegate that authority as specified in the manual.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1001, a Part 121 certificate holder may satisfy flight-crew flight-time and duty limitations either by complying with GACAR Part 117 or by implementing —
options: A fatigue risk management system (FRMS) providing an equivalent level of safety
A doubled minimum rest period only
No alternative — Part 117 is the sole option
