questions.explain: GACAR §121.1001 lets a certificate holder either comply with the Part 117 flight-time and duty limitations or implement a comprehensive FRMS, approved by the President, that provides an equivalent level of safety.

banks.title: Transport Performance & Dispatch
banks.desc: Performance, weather minima and dispatch requirements for transport-category operations (GACAR Part 121).
banks.source: GACAR Part 121 (performance & dispatch) · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Which subpart of GACAR Part 121 prescribes airplane performance operating limitations for all certificate holders?
questions.explain: Subpart F prescribes airplane performance operating limitations; Subpart G covers rotorcraft performance, Subpart M covers dispatcher qualifications, and Subpart Q covers records and reports.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.263, in computing takeoff distance the length of any clearway that may be included must not be greater than —
options: One-quarter the length of the runway
One-half the length of the runway
The full length of the runway
One-and-a-half times the length of the runway
questions.explain: §121.263 provides that the takeoff distance must not exceed the runway plus any clearway, but the clearway credited must not be greater than one-half the length of the runway.

questions.q: Under the destination landing limitations of GACAR §121.275, a turbojet-powered airplane's mass at takeoff must allow a full-stop landing within what portion of the effective runway length at the destination?
questions.explain: §121.275 requires that the anticipated landing mass allow a full-stop landing within 60 percent of the effective length of the runway (measured from 50 ft above the runway) for turbojet airplanes at the destination.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.275, if weather reports indicate the destination runways may be wet or slippery, a turbojet airplane may take off only if the effective runway length is at least what percentage of the dry-runway length otherwise required?
questions.explain: §121.275 requires that, for wet or slippery destination runways, the effective runway length be at least 115 percent of the length required for dry conditions, unless a shorter approved wet distance is in the AFM.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.279, an aerodrome may be listed as an alternate for a turbopropeller-powered airplane only if the aircraft can be brought to a full stop within what portion of the effective runway length?
questions.explain: §121.279 permits listing an alternate for a turbopropeller airplane only if a full-stop landing can be made within 70 percent of the effective runway length (60 percent for turbojet airplanes).

questions.q: GACAR §121.271 (en-route limitations, two engines inoperative) allows compliance by ensuring no point along the intended track is more than how long — with all engines operating at cruising power — from an adequate aerodrome?
questions.explain: One compliance option in §121.271 is that no place along the intended track is more than 90 minutes (all engines at cruising power) from an aerodrome meeting §121.279.

questions.q: Under the en-route limitation for one engine inoperative (GACAR §121.267), the airplane's mass must permit a net flight path clearing terrain and obstructions within the specified corridor by at least —
questions.explain: §121.267 requires (for the option reaching a landing aerodrome) that the one-engine-inoperative net flight path clear all terrain and obstructions within 4.4 NM of track by at least 2,000 ft vertically.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1309, no flight under the dispatch release system may start unless —
options: The pilot-in-command alone signs the load manifest
An aircraft dispatcher specifically authorizes that flight
The airplane has departed within the previous hour
questions.explain: §121.1309 provides that no person may start a flight unless an aircraft dispatcher specifically authorizes it, and the PIC and dispatcher are jointly responsible for preflight planning and dispatch release.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1309, a flight may not continue from an intermediate aerodrome without a re-dispatch if the aircraft has been on the ground more than —
questions.explain: §121.1309 requires re-dispatch to continue a flight from an intermediate aerodrome when the aircraft has been on the ground more than 6 hours.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1369, an aircraft may be dispatched or released for operations under IFR or over-the-top only when appropriate weather reports or forecasts indicate that the weather at the destination will be —
options: At or above the authorized minimums at the estimated time of arrival
VFR at all times en route
questions.explain: §121.1369 permits IFR/over-the-top dispatch or release only when weather reports or forecasts indicate conditions will be at or above the authorized minimums at the estimated time of arrival at the destination.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1393, a flight conducted under IFR or over-the-top must generally have how many destination alternate aerodromes specified in the dispatch or flight release?
options: None if the flight is under 3 hours
One alternate only for extended over-water flights
questions.explain: §121.1393 requires at least one destination alternate aerodrome be selected and specified for IFR or over-the-top flights, subject to limited exceptions (e.g., favorable forecast ceiling/visibility windows or isolated aerodromes).

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1397, no aerodrome may be listed as an alternate in a dispatch or flight release unless the appropriate weather reports or forecasts indicate the weather will be —
options: At or above the alternate weather minimums in the operations specifications when the flight arrives
Below landing minimums but with an ILS available
questions.explain: §121.1397 permits listing an alternate only when the weather is forecast to be at or above the alternate weather minimums in the certificate holder's operations specifications at the time the flight arrives.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1389, when a takeoff alternate is required, for an airplane having two engines the takeoff alternate must be located no more than —
options: 1 hour from the departure aerodrome at one-engine-inoperative cruise speed
2 hours from the departure aerodrome at all-engine cruise speed
questions.explain: §121.1389 requires a two-engine airplane's takeoff alternate to be within 1 hour of the departure aerodrome at the normal one-engine-inoperative cruising speed (ISA, still air, actual takeoff mass); three-or-more-engine airplanes get 2 hours at all-engine cruise.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1365, an aircraft may be dispatched or released for VFR operation only if the ceiling and visibility en route —
options: Are forecast to improve after departure
Are and will remain at or above applicable VFR minimums until arrival at the specified aerodrome(s)
Are reported at the departure aerodrome only
questions.explain: §121.1365 permits VFR dispatch or release only when weather reports or forecasts show the ceiling and visibility en route are and will remain at or above applicable VFR minimums until the aircraft reaches the aerodrome(s) specified.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1349, before releasing (or beginning) a flight, the aircraft dispatcher or PIC must —
options: Be thoroughly familiar with reported and forecast weather conditions on the route to be flown
File a written risk assessment with the President
questions.explain: §121.1349 requires the aircraft dispatcher (dispatch release system) or the PIC (flight release system) to be thoroughly familiar with reported and forecast weather conditions on the route to be flown before releasing/beginning the flight.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1341, a flight may not be allowed to continue to the aerodrome to which it was released unless the weather at a specified alternate is forecast to be —
options: At or above the alternate minimums in the operations specifications at the time the aircraft would arrive there
Clear of clouds regardless of visibility
The same as the departure aerodrome
questions.explain: §121.1341 prohibits continuing a flight unless the weather at a specified alternate is forecast to be at or above the alternate minimums in the operations specifications when the aircraft would arrive; the release may be amended en route to add an alternate within fuel range.

questions.q: GACAR §121.89 addresses ETOPS communications facilities. What does it primarily require of the certificate holder?
options: Provide voice communications for ETOPS where such facilities are available, considering diversion routes and altitudes
Install a second HF radio in every airplane
Restrict ETOPS to daylight hours only
Carry a satellite phone for each passenger
questions.explain: §121.89 requires the certificate holder to provide voice communications for ETOPS where facilities are available, considering potential routes and altitudes needed for diversion to ETOPS alternate aerodromes, substituting an approved system where voice is not possible.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1401, an aircraft may be dispatched or released for an ETOPS flight only if —
options: The destination weather is forecast VFR
Enough ETOPS alternate aerodromes are listed so the aircraft remains within the authorized ETOPS maximum diversion time
Every alternate has a precision approach
questions.explain: §121.1401 requires that enough ETOPS alternate aerodromes be listed in the dispatch or flight release so the aircraft remains within the authorized ETOPS maximum diversion time, selecting from adequate aerodromes meeting the standards of the part.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1409, for ETOPS up to and including 180 minutes, an aerodrome may not be listed as an ETOPS alternate if the time needed to reach it exceeds the aircraft's most limiting ETOPS significant system time minus —
questions.explain: §121.1409 provides that, for ETOPS up to 180 minutes, an aerodrome cannot be an ETOPS alternate if the flight time to it (one-engine-inoperative cruise, standard conditions) exceeds the most limiting ETOPS significant system time minus 15 minutes.

questions.q: Under the ETOPS fuel-supply rule (GACAR §121.1417), the aircraft must carry fuel to hold at 1,500 ft above field elevation upon reaching an ETOPS alternate for how long, then conduct an approach and land?
questions.explain: §121.1417 requires ETOPS fuel sufficient to hold at 1,500 ft above field elevation for 15 minutes upon reaching an ETOPS alternate aerodrome and then conduct an instrument approach and land, in addition to diversion, wind, icing, and deterioration allowances.

questions.q: Under GACAR §121.1417, when calculating ETOPS diversion fuel the certificate holder must increase the forecast wind speed by what margin to account for wind-forecasting errors?
questions.explain: §121.1417 requires the actual forecast wind speed to be increased by 5 percent (increasing headwind or decreasing tailwind) — or additional reserve fuel equal to 5 percent of the diversion fuel — to account for potential wind-forecasting errors.
